results.title: Everybody in the Place, An Incomplete History of Britain 1984-1992
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Single channel video

Duration: 1 hour 1 minute 35 seconds

Courtesy of the artist and The Modern Institute, Glasgow
results.title_for_label: Everybody in the Place, An Incomplete History of Britain 1984-1992
results.description:
Though the setting feels unremarkable, the history presented in this classroom explores one of Britain’s most transformative youth movements.

In Everybody in the Place, artist Jeremy Deller revisits the social and political forces that shaped the UK’s so-called Second Summer of Love (1988-89). The film is structured around a lecture he gave to political science students. Using rare archive footage, it connects acid house and rave culture with unemployment, police tensions and the 1984–85 miners’ strike – a major national conflict that changed Britain’s political direction.

House music travelled from clubs in Chicago and Detroit to Britain, and merged with British-Caribbean sound system culture. It moved into nightclubs, warehouses and open fields. Acid house, and later rave, did not appear from nowhere. It grew in a country that felt divided, giving young people new ways to gather, dance and build community as older systems were breaking apart.
description_for_label:
Though the setting feels unremarkable, the history presented in this classroom explores one of Britain’s most transformative youth movements.

results.title: Black To Techno
results.credit_line:
Single channel video

Duration: 20 minutes 25 seconds

Courtesy of the artist
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Single channel video

Duration: 20 minutes 25 seconds

Courtesy of the artist
results.description:
Detroit is known as both Motor City and Motown. For many years, car factories and music scenes grew side by side. As factory work changed to fast assembly lines, new communities formed and new rhythms began to shape the city. Machines moved in steady patterns. Later, drum machines and sequencers did the same. Work, movement and music became closely connected.

In Black to Techno, British Nigerian artist Jenn Nkiru tells this story using radio clips, interviews, home videos and archival footage. The film is layered like a piece of music, building a picture of how techno grew from Detroit’s Black working-class communities.

The Black church also influenced the city’s sound, with choirs, organs and call-and-response singing filling the air with energy and spirit. Tracing links between manufacturing, migration, worship and music, Nkiru shows that techno wasn’t an escape from factory life. It transformed it. She calls the film a “cosmic archaeology”, exploring how techno carries memory, hope and shared imagination into the future.
description_for_label:

results.title: Luanda–Kinshasa
results.credit_line:
Single channel video

Duration: 6 hours

Courtesy of the artist, Victoria Miro and David Zwirner
results.headline_credit_for_label:
Single channel video

Duration: 6 hours

Courtesy of the artist, Victoria Miro and David Zwirner
results.description:
A band is in the middle of a jam session. Keyboards, guitars, drums, percussion and saxophone lock into a layered rhythm that loops, builds and shifts. A sound engineer listens from the booth while friends, journalists and record label staff watch.

The scene unfolds inside a reconstruction of Columbia Records’ legendary New York studio, known as The Church. In the 1960s and 70s it hosted landmark recordings by Bob Dylan, Pink Floyd and Miles Davis. The musicians draw on African diasporic music that was moving through New York at the time, including Afrobeat songs like O Boso by Manu Dibango. They blend jazz, rock and African rhythms into something new.

Canadian artist Stan Douglas constructs a speculative history: crafting a six-hour remix by sampling two compositions, ‘Luanda’ and ‘Kinshasa’. Through editing and synchronisation, musicians appear to collaborate across time and space, showing how sound and technology can reorganise history into new cultural possibilities.
description_for_label:

results.title: The Cute Machine
results.creator_credit: Martine Corompt and Australian Film Commission
results.brief_description:
The Cute Machine is an exploration into the morphology of cute characterisation and iconography and its relationship to the biological world, seeking to analyse this ‘Cute ideal’ by correlating it with the Zoological process known as Neoteny

The CD contains two sections that had already been exhibited as installations (Sorry! and Activity Station) but were then adapted to a screen version. The remaining sections elaborate on the themes of cute iconography and its analysis.

The Cute Machine proposes an environment where cute iconography and user friendliness have rampantly accelerated, and reduced computer interface design to the look and sophistication of an infant toy, while also taking the premise, Cute = young to the extreme, where the ‘Cutest’, becomes an abstracted mutation of the original sentiment.

constellations_primary.name: From cute to evil
constellations_primary.description: In The Cute Machine, players are rewarded by making characters with the most juvenile or baby-ish features. Explore how broader screen works uphold or disrupt the idea that characters with cute traits are inherently good and those without are evil.

results.description:
Artistic videogames are becoming common, but they’re not a new phenomenon. In the 1990s, Australian artist Martine Corompt made videogame artworks that blended biological theory with technological development to explore the concept of cuteness. One aspect that Corompt investigated is ‘neoteny’, a term that describes how some species retain juvenile, ‘cute’ traits in adulthood to make others feel protective of them.

She drew a parallel between neoteny and the changing aesthetic of tech in the 90s. Her early artworks were made on Amiga, but she noted that Apple computers began introducing faces to their interface in the 90s, and messages addressing the user directly, creating a sense of familiarity — the computer wasn’t just a tool, but a friend.

The Cute Machine morphs and mutates ‘cuteness’ to the extreme, blurring the boundaries between innocence and transgression.

Please be advised, this work contains cartoon violence, nudity and adult themes.
description_for_label:
The Cute Machine is an exploration into the morphology of cute characterisation and iconography and its relationship to the biological world, seeking to analyse this ‘Cute ideal’ by correlating it with the Zoological process known as Neoteny

The CD contains two sections that had already been exhibited as installations (Sorry! and Activity Station) but were then adapted to a screen version. The remaining sections elaborate on the themes of cute iconography and its analysis.

The Cute Machine proposes an environment where cute iconography and user friendliness have rampantly accelerated, and reduced computer interface design to the look and sophistication of an infant toy, while also taking the premise, Cute = young to the extreme, where the ‘Cutest’, becomes an abstracted mutation of the original sentiment.

results.title: Bieberchella
results.material_description: digital screen recording of @faithhtenorioo’s TikTok stream
results.brief_description: This performance turns Justin Bieber’s screen into a portrait of the early internet. As he sings with his younger self, memes like Double Rainbow and Deez Nuts do more than add humour. They evoke the online world that produced him: a culture of webcam confession, viral randomness and participatory watching. Double Rainbow captures the raw sincerity of early YouTube, while Deez Nuts represents its throwaway, repeatable joke logic. Bieber belongs to both. He was one of the first pop stars built through that ecosystem, where intimacy and exposure fed each other. By folding memes into the set, he reframes his career as inseparable from the internet’s gaze.

results.title: Flesh on glass
results.creator_credit: Ann Turner, Lucy MacLaren and Swinburne Institute of Technology. Film and Television School
results.brief_description: Kate’s love for her brother’s wife and the ensuing feelings of guilt lead her to an obsession with the supernatural. We follow her struggle towards self-awareness.

results.title: Tit for tat [Censored version]
results.creator_credit: Martha Ansara, Graeme Isaac, Film Australia and Mayfan
results.brief_description: Examines the world of tattoos and the surrounding culture of bikers and rebels as the filmmaker travels with Baldy, a biker and photo-journalist, to the Sandhurst Rally for the yearly Tattoo Exhibition and judging. There they meet AJ, the most tattooed man in Australia and a renowned tattoo artist himself. WARNING - SOME SCENES IN THIS PROGRAMME MAY OFFEND SOME VIEWERS.

results.title: Perth's Mitchell Freeway
results.creator_credit: Norman A. Uren and Western Australian Government Film Unit
results.brief_description: The application of the Mitchell Freeway plan to Perth, its immediate advantage in handling growing traffic densities and the long term implications of city development, are seen in their close inter-relationships.
results.creator_credit_for_label: Norman A. Uren and Western Australian Government Film Unit

results.title: Le Voyage dans la Lune (A Trip to the Moon)
results.brief_description: Melies’ fantastical vision of life on the moon.

constellations_other.name: Space, sci-fi and cinema fakes
constellations_other.description: Magic lantern slides were used to entertain and educate audiences in home, theatres and universities. While performers told jokes and fairytales, travelling showmen unveiled exotic locations and scientists revealed the latest discoveries.

results.title_for_label: Le Voyage dans la Lune (A Trip to the Moon)
results.description: One of the first Science Fiction films, this Melies’ short humorously proposes what man discovers when he first lands on the moon. Based on the Jules Verne story, George Melies used his extraordinary cinematic illusions to tell this story of the first voyage to the moon. Made in 1902, this was a visionary film that has come to be recognised as a cinematic masterpiece. Among the many comical set pieces is the famous scene in which a rocket ship crash-lands into the eye of the ‘man in the moon’. This whimsical fantasy is also exemplary in its early depiction of the moon and the possible encounters between humans and worlds beyond our imagining, in our exploration of deep space.
description_for_label: One of the first Science Fiction films, this Melies’ short humorously proposes what man discovers when he first lands on the moon. Based on the Jules Verne story, George Melies used his extraordinary cinematic illusions to tell this story of the first voyage to the moon. results.title: [Cinesound Review 1950-1965]
results.brief_description:
This film contains excerpts from Cinesound Review, a news broadcast that covered significant social, cultural and political events in Australia. The film features a number of different clips from newsreels between 1950 and 1965.

The first section, titled ‘1950 Melbourne Cup’, shows the horse race at Flemington Racecourse, photographed by Ron Horner. The film includes close-ups of people in the crowd, and commentary of the race as Comic Court wins. This is followed by a section titled ‘Scone Rodeo: Bronchos (sic) on the Bust’, featuring footage of a horse rodeo in Scone, New South Wales.

The third section, titled ‘Fur Flies: Battling Budgerigar K.O.’s Cat’ features a playfight between two pets, a ginger cat and a budgerigar. In the fourth section we see ANZAC Day parades in Melbourne and Sydney, preluded by the title ‘ANZAC: Nation’s Homage’.

The fifth section is titled ‘Skiing: Day and Night Fun at Mt. Buller’, with commentary by David Low. It features children learning how to ski and social activities in the resorts, including bands and dancing. This is followed by an excerpt called ‘Clark Well Ahead’, showing the motorcar race at the 1965 British Grand Prix, won by Jim Clark.

The film ends with a cartoon called ‘Local Boy Makes Good: A Story with a Moral’ and three feature film trailers: ‘The Counterfeit Traitor’ (1962), ‘Pollyanna’ (1960) and ‘Mein Kampf’ (1960).
